start.text:
The expedition file says NEW at the top, and under it, in two hands that have learned to share a page, the plan: east along Pin's quiet lanes to the rail cutting, over the iron footbridge at Sedge Lane, up the ridge for the long look, and — if and only if the long look permits — down to the fence-inside-a-fence itself. Recon, not contact. Osy has said 'recon, not contact' four times, which you recognize as a woman instructing herself.

You leave in the grey before dawn, loaded like mules, B in his working harness with his own panniers — he carries his fish and his dignity, evenly balanced — and the city gives you its strange mercies as the sun comes up: a block where it is gently snowing out of a clear sky, the flakes crossing a seam and becoming warm rain mid-fall; a street of flowering chestnuts running some old May at quarter speed, petals hanging in the air like held breath.

Pin's lanes work. That is the eerie thing. Her hand-drawn corridors, plotted by a dead woman with a child's barometer, thread the drift like a pilot's channel markers — you cross seams only where she says crossable, and the cold hedges part at exactly her tolerances, and twice Osy touches the notebook in her jacket without seeming to know she has done it, the way another woman would touch a saint's medal.

At the lip of the rail cutting, the city opens. Below: the wide grey braid of the old freight lines, running east. Beyond, on the far bank, the ridge, and the monitoring hut you already know, and past it — visible now for the first time with your own eyes, low and long in the young light, moored inside its double fence like a ship in still water —

The Anchorage.

Even from here you can see the wrongness, and the wrongness is that there is none. Every roofline true. Every fence post straight. In a city of mismatched days, the yard below is the only thing in any direction that looks like it was built yesterday and will still be there tomorrow — a place the weather goes around, a still point wearing the plainness of infrastructure the way vaults wear the plainness of banks.

'There it is,' Osy says quietly. 'The middle of the middle.' And then, because the schedule is load-bearing: 'Bridge first. Long look from the ridge. Recon —'

'— not contact,' you say, and B exhales through his nose, which all three of you elect to count as a vote.

choices.text: The Sedge Lane footbridge, then — over the cutting, dead on Pin's line, the only crossing for a mile in either direction.
One instrument check at the treeline first — batteries, glasses, the receiver's gain — because the middle of the middle is not a place this household arrives at underequipped. Then the footbridge.

bridge_cross.text:
The Sedge Lane footbridge is a Victorian leftover, iron lattice and rivets, and it hums very slightly under the wind off the cutting — a long C-sharp, the sound of a structure that has been doing one job for a hundred and forty years and intends to continue.

B stops at the first tread.

Not the plant — not the Fennel Street refusal, the becoming-geological. This is different, and you have no file for it: he sets one paw on the iron, takes it off, sets it on again, and looks up at you both with his ears half-back, which in his language is a document too — not no; something more like a man signing a contract he has read twice and still doesn't like.

'Storm?' Osy asks, scanning east. The sky is quilted but calm; the drift readings off your wrist and pocket clocks are the mildest of the morning. Nothing shines. Nothing leans. Whatever the dog is countersigning, it is not on any instrument you carry.

You crouch and look down his sight-line, the borrowed instrument, and get nothing: the bridge, the far bank, the ridge path. He is not looking at a thing, you realize. He is looking at the crossing itself — at the act, not the place, the way he once looked at the lead in your hand before a walk he wasn't sure you meant to finish.

'Recurrence n of one,' you say, logging it aloud, because the protocol survives even when the categories fail. 'New behavior. No storm signature. Advise?'

Osy looks at the dog a long moment, then at the bridge, then at the four-mile detour south to the next crossing, and does the arithmetic you are both always doing now — data against daylight, caution against the cold. 'We cross,' she says finally. 'Together, fast, and we log it. If the bridge is wrong, it's wrong in a way nothing else can see, and that —' she settles her pack — 'is exactly the category of thing we came east to find.'

You cross. The iron hums its C-sharp under your boots, a hundred and forty years of one job. B crosses last, quickly, close to your knee, and at the far side he turns once and looks back along the span with his ears at half, as if fixing it in some register of his own — and then the ridge path takes you, and the morning moves on, and none of you knows yet that you have just done a thing that, by nightfall, will never have been possible.

choices.text: Up the ridge to the long look — bellies in the grass, glasses out, the Anchorage laid out below like a problem set.
Cross last, behind the dog and the colleague, watching the water in the cutting run its wrong direction — someone should hold the rear, and the job is yours by temperament.

long_look.text:
You spend the middle of the day on your bellies at the ridge line, in the dry grass by the monitoring hut, doing the thing you are both actually for: looking carefully at something for a long time.

The Anchorage rewards patience the way a locked door rewards a locksmith. Facts, accumulating in two notebooks: The double fence is not two fences; the inner one is newer, finer, and — through the glasses, where its line crosses a puddle — it does not reflect. The yard between the fences is swept gravel, and the sweep marks are fresh, and there are no footprints in it, anywhere, at all. Four long low sheds, roofs true, and a fifth structure in the center that is wrong in the way the whole place is wrong — too plain, a concrete barn with no doors on any side you can see, around which the other buildings sit in a horseshoe like courtiers who have been told not to turn their backs.

No people. No smoke, no wash lines, no glint of a sentry's glass. And yet: swept gravel. Oiled hinges on the hut behind you. Somebody keeps this place the way a verger keeps a church — invisibly, devotedly, between services.

And it is quiet. Not abandoned-quiet; engineered-quiet. The wind crosses the yard and comes out the other side, you would swear, combed. B lies between you in the grass, chin on paws, doing his own surveillance, and every few minutes a shiver walks his spine from tail to collar and is gone — his seam-shiver, but continuous, low-grade, as if the whole yard below were one long gentle seam that never stops.

'Readings,' Osy murmurs, eye to the glasses. 'Then the decision.'

choices.text: [Signal 3] Rig the receiver for a passive sweep — a place that hums like one long seam will leak something, and you have spent a year learning this city's static by heart.
[Grit 2] Work around the cutting's rim to the old water tower and climb it — thirty metres of rusted ladder buys the one angle the ridge can't: down, over the inner fence, into the horseshoe.
Take the ridge's data and be content: plot the drift gradient along the fence line with the clocks you trust. Patient, boring, safe — the survey method that found this place to begin with.

telemetry_sweep.text:
You build the sweep the way you built everything in the bad year: quietly, with your back against something solid.

Antenna low in the grass, gain up, the receiver breathing the yard's air across every band you own. For an hour it gives you what the city always gives — mesh-node heartbeats, the far bakery of the ionosphere — and then, patient as sediment, the yard's own voice settles out of the noise, and your skin does something it has not done since the first LOCK.

The Anchorage is transmitting. Continuously, at the bottom of the band, at a power level engineered to drown in static unless someone lay in dry grass for an hour with a year of this city's silence memorized. It is not voice. It is not any telemetry you know. It is a slow, ramified, self-similar clicking — patterned like a mechanical process reporting its own state, branch by branch, and the branches have branches, and by the second hour you have seen the pattern's skeleton and you put down the headphones and lie still, looking at the quilted sky, doing the thing where you refuse a conclusion until it forces you.

It forces you. You have seen this structure before — not heard, seen: on Osy's wall, in her own hand, red thread and pins. Decision trees. The yard is speaking in decision trees — positions, branches, evaluations, pruned lines dying with a terminal click like a tapped king — an endless quiet enumeration of futures, somewhere under that doorless concrete barn, running and running and running.

You pass Osy the headphones without a word. She listens for four minutes, and her face does what it did at the corner table eleven years ago: closes around a position, completely.

'It's playing,' she says, very quietly. 'Colleague. It's mid-game.'

item.name: The Yard's Voice
item.description: Two hours of passive sweep, one conclusion at the bottom of the band: the Anchorage transmits a continuous lattice of branching, self-pruning clicks. Decision trees, enumerating futures. Whatever lives under the doorless barn, it is mid-game.

choices.text: Enough. That word — mid-game — has changed the shape of the day. Pull back off the ridge line and hold the decision council.
Listen sixty seconds longer with the gain flat — logging the cadence, the tempo, evidence over adrenaline — and then pull back off the ridge line for the council.

tower_climb.text:
The water tower is four hundred metres south along the rim, and the ladder is a hundred and forty rungs of rust held together by paint and precedent, and you climb it because the body you have been deliberately hardening is finally worth spending.

Osy anchors the base with the rope from the Glasshouse; B supervises from the bottom with his ears at maximum, filing formal objections at intervals. The wind up top is a different animal, and you clip to the rail and breathe, and then you put the glasses down over the inner fence, into the horseshoe, and you earn the day's wage.

Down inside the courtiers' ring, hidden from every ground angle, the concrete barn has a door after all. In the roof. A flat rectangular section, seams too clean, flush as a hatch — and gravel below it, and here is the thing that empties your lungs: the sweep marks in that gravel radiate from the barn. Not toward. From. Whoever tends this place comes up out of the ground, sweeps their own footprints back to the hatch behind them, and closes the lid, and the pattern of the radii says they have done it hundreds of times, at intervals, for years.

And on the barn's flat roof, faint but unmistakable at this angle with the low sun raking it: paths. Worn into the concrete itself, the pale polish of repeated passage — a loop, from hatch to roof-edge and back, the exact pacing-groove of a man who climbs up out of the ground and walks his circuit and looks out at the broken city and goes back down. You know that wear. You have seen it once before, on the outside edge of a pair of slippers, in a room where you woke up eleven months ago wondering how long you had lived there.

Whoever keeps this vault walks like you.

You climb down with your hands steadier than they have any business being, and you report all of it — the hatch, the radii, the pacing-groove — in full, per the deal, out loud. All of it except the slippers. There is no way to say the slippers that does not sound like the fear talking, you tell yourself, and you will spend a long time, later, examining that sentence.

item.name: The Roof Hatch
item.description: The doorless barn has a door in its roof, sweep marks radiating outward from it, and a pacing-groove worn into the concrete — the circuit of someone who comes up out of the ground, walks, looks at the broken city, and closes the lid. Hundreds of visits. Years of them.

choices.text: Down, and back along the rim, and hold the decision council — the yard has a keeper, and the keeper lives underneath it.
Sketch the hatch and the radii on your knee before descending — the pacing-groove measured, the slippers unmentioned — and carry the drawing down to the council.

gradient_survey.text:
You do it the boring way, which is the way that found this place, and the boring way pays its usual honest wage.

The afternoon goes to the drift gradient: clock pairs read at hundred-metre intervals along the ridge, the disagreement between your honest timepieces mapped against distance from the fence. The numbers build a curve, and the curve is the strangest thing either of you has ever plotted, and you have both spent a season plotting the end of the world.

Drift falls as you approach the yard. Smoothly. Continuously. Like temperature toward a cold spring, like noise toward an anechoic door — and at the fence line itself, extrapolated, it reaches zero. Not low. Zero. Inside that wire, the readings say, there is no drift at all: one day, the same day, the true day, held steady while the whole city around it runs to quilt. You check the curve three times because it is too clean; instruments are never this polite; the world, in your experience, does not draw its own diagrams.

'It's not that the weather goes around,' Osy says slowly, watching you plot the third confirmation. 'That's what I had. Deflection. Defense.' She shakes her head, eyes on the curve. 'This is worse. This is a gradient. The yard isn't dodging the broken time, it's — draining it. Flattening it. Everything for a kilometre is calmer because that place is holding the day still, the way a nail holds cloth.' She looks up, over the grass, at the low true rooflines. 'And things that hold cloth against a pull, colleague. They have names. In sails, in tents, in ships.' A silence. 'You moor things to an anchor.'

B, lying between you, shivers his long low continuous shiver, tail to collar, at the one long gentle seam that never stops.

item.name: The Zero Curve
item.description: Drift versus distance, three confirmations: broken time smooths as you approach the fence and extrapolates to zero inside it. The Anchorage is not dodging the weather of days. It is holding the day still, the way a nail holds cloth.

choices.text: Curve in hand, hold the decision council: the long look is done, and the question is how much closer 'recon' goes.
Walk the seam's line one careful stretch further, curve in hand, until the word moor stops being a metaphor — then the council, and the question of how much closer recon goes.

decision_point.text:
The council convenes at the monitoring hut in the late gold light: two humans, one dog, one decision.

The case for the fence: you are here, the daylight holds, and every hour of today has made the yard more answerable and less imaginary. The case against is Osy's own rule, said four times, and the tube in your pack with your own handwriting saying not yet, and the fact — which none of you says, and all of you have — that B has not fully settled since the bridge, a low idle running under his coat all day.

Osy spreads her master notebook on the chart table — the current one, thirty-ninth of forty-one, the wall of red thread distilled into pages — and works the decision the way she works everything, out loud, branch by branch. 'Contact today: we learn fast, and if the keeper is hostile we learn that fast too, four miles from home, at dusk. Contact never: the ellipse stays a shape and the shape eventually comes to her — to us,' and the slip is small and neither of you touches it, and it lies on the chart table between you like a dropped stitch. 'Contact prepared: we go home, we work the substation angle, we come back with a plan that doesn't improvise the first meeting with the thing that aims storms.'

She caps her pen. 'Branch three. Recon, not contact — the rule held all day; it holds at dusk. Objections?'

B stands up.

Not at you. Not at the yard. East, past the hut, at the quilted horizon, where absolutely nothing is visible — and his whole body has gone to the drawn-bow line, and the idle under his coat has become the thing itself: ears flat, weight forward, tail rigid, and then, for the first time in the eleven months and the whole of his recorded life, B growls.

The sound is low and continuous and wrong in his throat, a door opening on a room the house never had, and it does not stop, and it is aimed at the empty east, and Osy is already sweeping instruments into her pack, because whatever the first growl in a dog's life means, it does not mean stay.

choices.text: Trust the dog with everything. Packs on, lights doused, west at speed — home ahead of whatever the east is about to be.

the_edit.text:
You are four hundred metres from the cutting when the east arrives, and it does not arrive as a storm.

There is no shine, no running brick, no cold hedge rolling up a street. There is instead a stillness that comes down over the evening like a hand over a bell — the wind stopping mid-gust, the grass upright, B going silent mid-growl as if the growl had been taken rather than finished — and then a sensation you will spend the rest of your life failing to describe and recognizing instantly: the feeling of a page being turned under you. Underneath everything. One tick of a mechanism too large to have a sound, translated to you through your bones' oldest listening.

Then the wind finishes its gust. The grass moves. The evening resumes, note-perfect, and nothing anywhere is different, and you stand in the resumed world with your heart going like a struck bird and no data at all — every clock agreeing, every reading nominal, the horizon in every direction exactly the quilted dusk it was.

'What was that,' Osy says. Not a question — a deposition. Her hand is white-knuckled on her pack strap. 'You felt it. Both of us. He —' B is turning in a slow circle, nose up, taking a census of a world he does not accept, and then he stops, facing west. Facing home. Facing the cutting, and his ears go down, and he makes a sound you have never heard from him either, small and bewildered, a query with no grammar.

You walk the four hundred metres with the dusk going down around you like a theater dimming, and you come to the lip of the cutting at Sedge Lane, and you stop, and the world quietly presents its invoice.

The footbridge is gone. Not wrecked, not fallen — gone the way a word is gone from a sentence that never had it. The stubs of its footings jut from the banks, rusted smooth, overgrown, decades overgrown, moss on the shear-faces, a bramble the size of a haystack rooted through the eastern anchor plate. On the far bank, where you ate lunch in its shadow, nettles stand in the shape of years of nothing. It did not fall tonight. It fell, the banks and the moss and the brambles all agree, long before the Halfway — it has always been fallen, this bridge you crossed eleven hours ago on iron humming C-sharp under three sets of feet.

B whines once, softly, looking at the gap. Then he looks up at you — at you, specifically, of the three of you at you — and the question in the look is the oldest question in his species' contract with yours, and for the first time ever, you cannot answer it.

choices.text: Say the scientist's sentence, out loud, before the vertigo eats it: 'We remember a bridge. The world doesn't. One of those records has been edited.' Then find other witnesses — the handcart family camped down the rim.
Check the family first — the handcart family you passed camped by the cutting this morning. Other witnesses, before anything else. Data before dread.

witnesses.text:
The handcart family is camped where they were camped this morning, two hundred metres down the rim, and their fire is lit, and their existence is suddenly the most valuable thing on the ridge.

They watched you come up the eastern path this morning; the mother traded Osy a strip of dried apple for a weather word; the boy threw a stick that B declined with visible regret, being in harness. If the bridge was, they saw you cross it. If the bridge wasn't, they saw you appear on a bank you had no way to reach.

The mother listens to Osy's question — asked lightly, masterfully, a traveler confused about her own route — and shakes her head with the mild certainty of the local. 'Bridge at Sedge Lane? No, love. Been down since before the trouble. Went in the bad winter — I was a girl. We heard it go, whole valley did, like a church falling over.' She says it warmly, unhurriedly, a woman repeating a thing as true as the river: a memory, forty years deep, of a night that — until this evening, by every record you trust — never happened. 'You'll have come over the aqueduct like everyone, and lost your bearings in the quilt. It does that.'

The boy, though. While the mother redraws your morning for you, the boy is looking at B, frowning, working something in the private court where children try the world's claims. 'He crossed on the bridge,' the boy says, finally, to nobody, in the flat voice of testimony. 'The dog. I saw the dog on the bridge. He didn't want to.' And then his mother's hand finds his shoulder — gentle, absent, correcting — and something crosses his small face like the shadow of a page turning, and he blinks, and says, 'Off the aqueduct, I mean,' and goes back to his stick.

You watch it happen. You watch the edit finish its housekeeping in a child's face by firelight, forty seconds late, and of everything the day has shown you, it is this — the seam closing in real time, the small voice correcting itself into the new true — that finally puts the cold all the way through you.

Osy thanks the mother for the weather word and walks away steady as a metronome, twenty paces, thirty, out of the firelight, and then stops in the dark and stands very still, and when you reach her she says, in a voice scoured down to its load-bearing members: 'It got the boy in under a minute, and it never got us at all. Write that down, colleague. Whatever this is — it can't reach us. And it has to know that. So the question stops being what happened tonight.' She turns east, toward the black horseshoe of the yard, invisible now against the risen dark. 'The question is what we are, that we're allowed to keep the old day. And who does the allowing. And I think you and I have both begun to have a suspicion about where they live.'

choices.text: South to the aqueduct crossing — the long dark way home, with the new true world all around you and the old one riding in exactly two skulls and possibly one dog.
One look back at the mother and the boy before the dark takes the bank — the first two entries in a ledger you are only beginning to suspect you keep — then south, the long way home.

far_bank.text:
The aqueduct takes you over the cutting a mile south — a crossing you have never used and the world insists you always have — and the walk home through the dark is the quietest four miles the three of you have ever done.

There is a specific silence for this and no name for it in any language from before: the silence of holding, in a two-person quorum, the entire minority record of the universe. Every step of the route home confirms the majority: the trail down from the aqueduct is worn, used, correct; your own morning's bootprints, which should thread the eastern path to the bridge, are simply not there, not anywhere, as if the morning had been recompiled with different assets. The city has closed over the old day like water over a stone. Only you are still wet with it.

Osy talks because she is Osy and this is how she carries weight — low, precise, hypothesis after hypothesis, stress-testing each one against the dark. Mass hallucination: fails on the dog. Localized desync, a pocket that held an old bridge-day and popped: fails on the boy, who remembered your crossing before he didn't. Instrument failure: there are no instruments for this; that is the point; the only instruments that dissented were memories.

'So it's the wall,' she says finally, as the station's ventilation shafts loom out of the dark like standing stones. 'Everything on my wall — the lucky mains, the timed collapses, the storms with taste. I kept saying preferences and letting everyone hear metaphor. Tonight wasn't a preference. Tonight was a decision, implemented, with cleanup. Something looked at a bridge that existed and decided it never had. And the reach of that decision covered a valley and a forty-year-old woman's whole childhood and a boy's eyes — and stopped at us.' She stops at the stair mouth, hand on the cold rail, and looks at you in the dark with the day's last question, the one that will not be going to bed. 'Reconstruct it. Why would a thing that can rewrite a valley leave two witnesses standing in the middle of it, remembering?'

B pushes past your legs and down the stairs, done with the day at last — and it is a measure of everything that his smallest ordinary rudeness, the shove of warm dog against your knee, lands like a mercy: one witness, at least, off the stand and gone to his blanket.

choices.text: Answer her honestly, at the foot of the stairs, with the only hypotheses that survive the night — and let her write them into the record.
Take the stairs first and put the kettle on — some testimony wants a table under it — and give her the honest hypotheses over tea, on the record.

notebook_scene.text:
You hold the council of two at the folding table, past midnight, tea gone cold, and you say the survivors out loud while she writes.

'Hypothesis one: we were out of range in some dimension we can't measure — the edit propagated through the world and we happened to be standing off the page. Testable, barely: it predicts witnesses like us exist after every edit, scattered, confused, learning to keep quiet. Hypothesis two —' and this one you have to walk at, slowly, because it has been standing at the edge of your mind's light since the firelight and the boy — 'we weren't skipped. We were spared. The edit saw us and went around, the way the storms go around the yard. Which predicts something that hypothesis one doesn't: that it knows us. Specifically. That somewhere in the machinery that turned tonight's page, there is a list, and we're on it.'

The pen stops. 'Say the third one,' Osy says quietly, not looking up. 'You've been not saying it since the fence line. I've watched you not say it all day.' And when you look at her: 'Colleague, I read your silences the way you read the dog's. Say it.'

So you say it. 'The handwriting in the hut is mine. The pacing wear on that roof is mine — I pace exactly that circuit, you've seen me, rim of the roof, look at the city, back. The survey in the tube used my method before I had invented it here, with you. Hypothesis three is that the list isn't a list.' The words come out in your flattest voice, and you understand, saying them, why she keeps her flattest voice for her most careful cargo. 'It's one name. And the reason the edits go around me is the reason storms don't hit the switching yard. You don't aim the weather at your own equipment.'

The lamp hisses. B twitches, dreaming, in the good dark past the light. And Osy closes her thirty-ninth notebook, and slides it across the table to you — the whole distilled wall, the year alone, the sister, the ellipse, everything — and puts her pen on top of it.

'Then as of tonight we keep the file in two skulls and two hands,' she says. 'Full copies. No abridging, no protecting each other from pages. Files with one copy don't survive — and neither, colleague, do witnesses who hide their working from each other. Whatever you are —' and her voice does not soften; it does something better; it levels, like a gaze meeting you at height — 'we find it the way we've found everything. Together, in the open, at full effort. Now write your day. All of it. I'll make the tea.'

item.name: Osy's Decision Trees
item.description: Her thirty-ninth notebook: the wall distilled — a year alone, the pins, the ellipse, Pin's storms, and tonight's bridge, in the minute vandal-proof hand. Given, not lent. Files with one copy don't survive.

choices.text: Write the day into her notebook in your own impatient hand — the bridge, the boy, the three hypotheses, the name — and let the household hold its new weight together.
Write the day, all of it — and when you reach the name, print it in block capitals, once, the way you would label a hazard, and keep writing.

end_ch5.text:
You write until the second pot of tea, and she reads over your shoulder without pretense, correcting nothing, and when it is down — all of it, in two hands in one book — the household is quiet in a new way, the quiet of a ship that has taken on its true cargo and sits lower and steadier in the water.

'Tomorrow,' Osy says, rinsing the pot, 'we work the perimeter of the problem, not the center. The substation angle. The Continuum — if those tunnel engineers are alive anywhere, somebody at the Glasshouse sells their mail. We build the file on the yard until the file tells us how to knock.' She racks the pot, and stands a moment with her back to you, hands flat on the counter that is really a crate, and the lamp throws her shadow long across the platform where her beacon called you out of the dark a month and a lifetime ago.

'One more entry,' she says, not turning around. 'For your side of the book. Tonight, at the cutting — when the world came up short a bridge, and every soul and stone in that valley agreed against us —' she does turn, now, and her face is the face from the fourth floor, the one you were never issued a name for — 'the only thing that held the old day steady was that there were two of us in it. I have been one witness for a year, colleague. I know exactly what it weighs. Whatever else gets edited —' and she taps the notebook, twice, her signature gavel — 'that line item stands. Two skulls. One file. Write it down.'

You write it down.

In the dark after the lamp, long past the dog's third sigh, you lie on your bunk doing the thing you do instead of sleeping now: walking the pacing-groove of the day. A bridge that never was. A boy's face closing like water. A yard that holds the day still the way a nail holds cloth. And pressed under everything, in handwriting you cannot argue with, the two words from the ridge, which have stopped being a message and become a schedule:

Not yet.

Almost, something in you answers, in your own voice, from very far down. And you lie in the dark examining that answer — its calm, its familiarity, its terrible fluency — until sleep takes the witness stand away.
